definition: The study of why efficient step-by-step procedures are vital for solving complex computational problems.
A collection of high-quality materials, books, and platforms for learning algorithmic techniques.
The creative and systematic process of breaking down a problem to find an optimal solution.
Various strategies and mental frameworks used to tackle different types of algorithmic challenges.
A computational measure that describes the amount of computer time it takes to run an algorithm.
The process of reorganizing a sequence of elements into a specific order (ascending or descending).
A search algorithm that finds the position of a target value within a sorted array by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half.
An algorithmic technique that uses two indices to iterate through a data structure, typically used for searching or interval problems.
Operations that manipulate individual bits of a binary number, such as AND, OR, XOR, and NOT.
Advanced tricks and clever applications of bitwise operations to optimize performance and memory.
A strategy that solves a problem by calling itself and undoing choices that do not lead to a solution.
An optimization technique that splits a search space into two halves, solves them independently, and combines the results.
A technique to map a large range of values into a smaller range while preserving their relative order.
A high-level look at how data can be organized, managed, and stored for efficient access and modification.
The most fundamental linear data structures used for storing collections of elements.
A linear data structure serving as a collection of elements with a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) access policy.
Techniques for performing efficient range sum queries and range updates on an array.
Using a double-ended queue to find the minimum or maximum element in a moving window over an array.
A complete binary tree that maintains the heap property, used to implement priority queues.
A data structure that maps keys to values using a hash function for high-speed data retrieval.
A data structure that answers range queries (like minimum/maximum) in O(1) time after O(N log N) preprocessing.
Data structures that preserve their previous versions when modified, allowing access to any version at any time.
A probabilistic data structure that allows fast search, insertion, and deletion within an ordered sequence of elements.
A technique used to jump through a data structure or tree in powers of two, often used for LCA and tree queries.
A tree data structure for storing information about intervals, supporting range queries and point/range updates.
A compact data structure (Binary Indexed Tree) that efficiently updates elements and calculates prefix sums.
A tree data structure specifically designed to hold intervals and find which intervals overlap with a given point or interval.
A specialized segment tree used to maintain a set of lines and find the maximum/minimum value at a specific x-coordinate.
A multi-dimensional data structure for storing points, allowing efficient range searching in higher dimensions.
A technique that divides a data structure into blocks of size sqrt(N) to perform range queries and updates efficiently.
General concepts and basic techniques for handling and searching text strings.
The Knuth-Morris-Pratt algorithm for finding all occurrences of a pattern string within a text efficiently.
A prefix tree data structure used for efficient retrieval of strings and solving string-based problems.
Mapping strings to integers to allow for fast comparison and substring identification.
An algorithm used to find all palindromic substrings in a string in linear time.
An array where each element Z[i] is the length of the longest common prefix between a string and its suffix starting at i.
A string-searching algorithm used to locate multiple patterns within a text simultaneously using a finite automaton.
A sorted array of all suffixes of a string, used for advanced string matching and analysis.
A powerful directed acyclic graph that represents all substrings of a given string.
Also known as Eertree, a data structure that stores all unique palindromic substrings of a string.
Learning the basics of solving complex problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems.
A classic DP problem involving selecting items with weights and values to maximize total value within a limit.
Using bitwise masks to represent states in DP, often for problems involving subsets or permutations.
A DP technique used to solve problems related to properties of digits of numbers within a range.
Applying DP techniques to hierarchical tree structures, often involving recursive transitions from children to parents.
An optimization technique for DP states that can be represented as lines, allowing for faster minimum/maximum queries.
A study of graphs, which are mathematical structures used to model pairwise relations between objects.
A data structure that tracks a set of elements partitioned into several non-overlapping (disjoint) subsets.
A graph traversal algorithm that explores all neighbor nodes at the present depth before moving to the next level.
A graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking.
A linear ordering of vertices such that for every directed edge uv, vertex u comes before v.
An algorithm for finding the shortest paths between nodes in a graph with non-negative edge weights.
A subset of the edges of a connected, edge-weighted graph that connects all the vertices together without any cycles and with the minimum possible total edge weight.
In a tree, the lowest node that has both given nodes as descendants.
A technique to decompose a tree into paths, allowing for efficient range queries and updates on tree paths.
A divide and conquer technique on trees that recursively finds the centroid to break the tree into smaller components.
Finding the greatest amount of flow that can be sent through a flow network from source to sink.
An algorithmic paradigm that builds up a solution piece by piece, always choosing the next piece that offers the most obvious and immediate benefit.
Fundamental concepts including GCD, LCM, and modular arithmetic.
An ancient and efficient algorithm for finding all prime numbers up to any given limit.
An integer x such that (a * x) is congruent to 1 modulo m.
A theorem used to solve systems of simultaneous congruences with different moduli.
A function that counts the positive integers up to n that are relatively prime to n.
The branch of mathematics dealing with combinations of objects belonging to finite sets.
A counting technique which computes the number of elements in a union of multiple sets.
The study of algorithms which can be stated in terms of geometry.
An algorithmic paradigm that uses a conceptual vertical line swept across the plane to solve various geometric problems.
The smallest convex shape that contains all the points in a given set on a plane.
An algorithm that computes the discrete Fourier transform (DFT) and its inverse, often used for fast polynomial multiplication.
The study of mathematical models of strategic interaction among rational agents, often applied to Nim and Grundy numbers.
An algorithm design paradigm based on multi-branched recursion.
